summary: Fowler initially appreciated C#'s property syntax as a cleaner alternative to Java's getter/setter conventions, noting that properties and fields share the same access syntax. This suggested developers could start with simple public fields and refactor to properties later when needed. However, he discovered that fields and properties have different reflection representations, meaning tools that use reflection break when switching between them. This forces developers to write accessor functions from the start, negating the promised benefit.
key_points: C#'s property syntax is more natural than Java's getX/setX conventions
Properties and fields share identical access syntax in program text, suggesting easy refactoring
Reflection treats fields and properties differently, breaking the promised interchangeability
This reflection mismatch forces defensive coding with accessor functions from the start
money_quotes: The getX and setX conventions of C++/Java always seems rather silly to me, it's much more natural to write obj.X = other.X.
I don't worry about this violating encapsulation, because should I wish to do something more fancy I can just replace it with a property later.
Fields and properties do have the same access in terms of program text, but they are represented differently by reflection.
So now I have to write stupid accessor functions for accessible data values. Sigh. (Or use Ruby.)
key_insight: Language features that appear equivalent in source code but differ at the reflection level create hidden refactoring barriers that undermine their intended benefits.
